questions.explanation: ISO 27035 defines an information security incident as an unwanted or unexpected single or series of information security events that have a significant probability of compromising business operations and threatening information security. This broad definition includes: malware infections, unauthorized access, data breaches, DoS attacks, policy violations, physical security failures, and accidental data disclosures. All significant information security events must be managed through the incident management process.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27035 IS Incident = Unwanted/unexpected events with significant probability of compromising IS or business operations

questions.question: What is 'security orchestration' and how does it improve compliance?
options: Organizing security staff into teams

Connecting and integrating security tools and processes so they work together automatically — enabling consistent policy enforcement, automated evidence collection, standardized incident response procedures, and compliance reporting across the security ecosystem
Conducting compliance audits in an organized manner
Playing orchestral music to security staff for focus
questions.explanation: Security orchestration connects disparate security tools (SIEM, firewall, EDR, ticketing, vulnerability scanner, etc.) through APIs and playbooks, enabling them to work together automatically. For compliance: it standardizes incident response (meeting NIST/ISO 27001 requirements), automates evidence collection for audits, enforces consistent policy across tools, and generates compliance reports automatically. SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) platforms implement this capability.
questions.takeaway: Security Orchestration = Connecting security tools via APIs/playbooks for automated, consistent response, evidence collection, and compliance reporting

questions.question: What is the 'principle of purpose limitation' in data protection law?
options: Limiting the processing speed of personal data
Limiting who can define data processing purposes

Personal data collected for one specific purpose must not be reused for incompatible new purposes without either obtaining fresh consent or identifying a compatible lawful basis — fundamental to GDPR, DPDPA, and modern privacy law
Limiting the data processing budget
questions.explanation: Purpose limitation (GDPR Article 5(1)(b); DPDPA 2023 Section 4) requires that personal data collected for a specified purpose cannot be further processed in ways incompatible with that purpose without consent or another lawful basis. For example, customer data collected for order fulfillment cannot be repurposed for marketing without additional consent. Compatibility assessment considers: link between purposes, context, data type, consequences for individuals, and safeguards.
questions.takeaway: Purpose Limitation = Data collected for purpose A cannot be reused for incompatible purpose B without fresh consent or lawful basis

questions.question: What is 'information security policy lifecycle'?
options: A NIST-defined policy management framework
The lifecycle of individual security policies from creation to deletion
The annual budget cycle for security

The continuous process of creating, reviewing, approving, communicating, implementing, monitoring, and updating information security policies — ensuring they remain current, effective, and aligned with the threat landscape and regulatory requirements
questions.explanation: The security policy lifecycle ensures policies remain effective: Create (identify need, draft policy), Review (legal/technical/stakeholder review), Approve (management/board approval), Communicate (awareness training, distribution), Implement (embed in processes), Monitor (compliance tracking, exception management), and Review/Update (periodic review cycle, update when regulations, threats, or business change). ISO 27001 requires regular policy reviews.
questions.takeaway: Policy Lifecycle = Create → Review → Approve → Communicate → Implement → Monitor → Review/Update (continuous cycle)

questions.question: What is 'penetration testing methodology' and what are the standard phases?
options: A structured approach including: Planning (scope, rules of engagement), Reconnaissance (passive/active information gathering), Scanning/Enumeration (port/service discovery, vulnerability scanning), Exploitation (attempting to exploit vulnerabilities), Post-exploitation (pivot, maintain access, data exfiltration simulation), and Reporting (documenting findings and recommendations)
A methodology for physical security testing only
A marketing methodology for security tool vendors
An automated scanning methodology
questions.explanation: A penetration testing methodology provides a structured, repeatable approach. Standard phases: Planning (define scope, objectives, rules of engagement, timeline); Reconnaissance (OSINT, passive/active information gathering); Scanning/Enumeration (port scanning, service identification, vulnerability scanning); Exploitation (attempt to exploit identified vulnerabilities); Post-exploitation (assess impact: privilege escalation, lateral movement, data access); and Reporting (document all findings with severity ratings and remediation recommendations).
questions.takeaway: Pen Testing Phases = Planning → Reconnaissance → Scanning → Exploitation → Post-exploitation → Reporting

questions.question: What does 'SSAE 18' (Statement on Standards for Attestation Engagements) govern?
options: Social security attestation requirements

The AICPA standard governing attestation engagements including SOC 1 reports (SSAE 18 replaced SSAE 16 in 2017) — setting requirements for how service organization controls reports are prepared and the CPA's responsibilities
Software security attestation requirements
State security assessment engagement requirements
questions.explanation: SSAE 18 (Statement on Standards for Attestation Engagements No. 18) is the AICPA standard that governs SOC 1 examinations (replacing SSAE 16 in 2017). It establishes requirements for the CPA's examination of management's description of a service organization's system and the suitability of design and (for Type 2) operating effectiveness of controls relevant to user entities' internal control over financial reporting. International equivalent is ISAE 3402.
questions.takeaway: SSAE 18 = AICPA standard governing SOC 1 examinations; replaced SSAE 16 in 2017; international equivalent is ISAE 3402

questions.question: What is 'ISAE 3000' and why is it relevant to SOC 2 internationally?
options: International Standards for Accounting Examinations
International Securities Assessment and Examination 3000

International Standard on Assurance Engagements 3000 — the international equivalent of SSAE 18 used by non-U.S. CPA firms for reporting on controls at service organizations — SOC 2 reports from non-U.S. firms may be prepared under ISAE 3000
Internet Security Attestation and Evaluation standard
questions.explanation: ISAE 3000 (Assurance Engagements Other than Audits or Reviews of Historical Financial Information) is the international standard issued by IAASB (International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board) for assurance engagements. For SOC 2-equivalent reports outside the U.S., CPA firms may use ISAE 3000 (for non-financial matters like security controls). Some reports are issued under both SSAE 18 and ISAE 3000 to satisfy both U.S. and international customers.
questions.takeaway: ISAE 3000 = International equivalent of SSAE 18; used by non-U.S. CPA firms for SOC 2-equivalent engagements

questions.question: What is 'compensating control documentation' under PCI DSS?
options: A list of all compensating controls used across the CDE
Documentation of all security control purchases
Documentation showing compensating controls are cheaper than required controls

A formal document required when using a compensating control — must describe the constraint that prevents meeting the original requirement, the intended objective of the original requirement, how the compensating control meets that objective, and risk acceptance by the responsible executive
questions.explanation: PCI DSS requires specific documentation for compensating controls: (1) Constraints — the legitimate technical or business constraint preventing implementation of the original requirement; (2) Objective — what the original requirement intended to achieve; (3) Identified risk — additional risk from not implementing the original control; (4) Definition of compensating control — how it meets the objective; (5) Validation — how it will be tested; and (6) Maintenance — how it will be maintained. This documentation must be approved by the QSA.
questions.takeaway: PCI DSS Compensating Control Documentation = Constraints + Objective + Risk + Control definition + Validation + Maintenance (QSA-approved)

questions.question: What is 'security baseline hardening' and how does it relate to CIS Benchmarks?
options: The process of configuring systems to a secure, known-good state by applying a security hardening standard — CIS Benchmarks provide specific, prescriptive hardening guidance for hundreds of platforms (OS, databases, network devices, cloud services, applications)
Baseline measurements of network security performance
The minimum security perimeter around a building
Only initial setup of brand-new systems
questions.explanation: Security baseline hardening involves configuring systems according to a documented security standard to eliminate unnecessary services, change default credentials, enable security features, and configure access controls — reducing the attack surface from its default (often insecure) state. CIS Benchmarks provide the most widely used prescriptive hardening guidance, with specific configurations for Windows, Linux, macOS, AWS, Azure, GCP, databases, browsers, and more.
questions.takeaway: Security Baseline Hardening = Configuring systems to secure state; CIS Benchmarks provide prescriptive guidance for 100+ platform types

questions.question: What is 'data retention schedule' and how does it relate to GDPR's storage limitation principle?
options: A schedule for data backup intervals

A documented policy specifying how long each category of personal and organizational data should be retained before being securely deleted or anonymized — implements GDPR's storage limitation principle by ensuring data is not kept longer than necessary for its purpose
A schedule for replacing data storage hardware
The schedule for backing up data to secondary storage
questions.explanation: A data retention schedule specifies the retention period for each data category — based on business need, legal requirements, and regulatory obligations (e.g., financial records: 7 years; employment records: varies by country; patient records: state law; GDPR documentation: 6 years from last effective date). It implements GDPR's storage limitation principle, ensuring personal data is deleted/anonymized when no longer needed for its specified purpose.
questions.takeaway: Data Retention Schedule = Documents how long each data category is kept; implements GDPR storage limitation (delete/anonymize when no longer needed)

questions.question: What is 'security incident communication' and why is it important?
options: Internal communication among security team members only
Communication between security tools via APIs

The structured process for communicating about security incidents — internally (to management, employees, legal, PR) and externally (to customers, regulators, law enforcement, media) — following predefined escalation criteria and communication templates to ensure timely, accurate, and controlled information sharing
Only relevant for public-facing companies
questions.explanation: Security incident communication includes: internal escalation (who gets notified at what incident severity level), management reporting (what information to provide at what frequency), external notifications (regulators within required timeframes — GDPR 72 hours, HIPAA 60 days, CERT-In 6 hours), customer notification (when their data is affected), and potentially media/public communication. Pre-defined communication templates and decision trees prevent errors during high-stress incidents.
questions.takeaway: Incident Communication = Predefined internal escalation + external notification (regulators, customers) with templates and decision trees

questions.question: What is 'GDPR Recital' and how do Recitals help interpret GDPR?
options: A type of consent mechanism
A formal reporting requirement under GDPR
Regulatory guidelines published after GDPR

Non-binding introductory paragraphs that explain the context, objectives, and interpretation of GDPR Articles — they are not legally binding themselves but are essential for understanding the intent behind GDPR's requirements and are referenced in regulatory decisions
questions.explanation: GDPR Recitals are the 173 numbered paragraphs that precede the Articles. They are not legally binding themselves but explain the legislative intent, provide examples, and clarify how Articles should be interpreted. For example, Recital 26 clarifies that anonymous data is outside GDPR's scope; Recital 49 explains that network security can be a legitimate interest. Supervisory authorities, courts, and the EDPB frequently cite Recitals in their decisions and guidance.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Recitals = 173 non-binding explanatory paragraphs providing interpretation context for GDPR Articles (frequently cited in regulatory decisions)

questions.question: What is 'ISO/IEC 27031' about?
options: Guidelines for information and communication technology (ICT) readiness for business continuity — extending ISO 22301 business continuity principles specifically to ICT systems and services
IT service quality management
Cloud service continuity standards
Physical security standards for ICT infrastructure
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27031 provides guidelines for concepts and principles of ICT (Information and Communication Technology) readiness for business continuity. It extends ISO 22301 by addressing the specific ICT aspects of business continuity management: ICT service continuity strategies, designing ICT for resilience, ICT incident response, recovery procedures for ICT, and testing ICT resilience. It helps organizations ensure ICT systems support business continuity during and after disruptions.
questions.takeaway: ISO/IEC 27031 = ICT readiness for business continuity (extends ISO 22301 to ICT-specific resilience and recovery)

questions.question: What is 'evidence-based auditing' vs 'compliance-based auditing'?
options: Evidence-based uses technical tools; compliance-based uses documentation

Evidence-based auditing focuses on testing whether controls actually work and risks are managed (outcomes-focused); compliance-based auditing focuses on whether documented requirements are met (checkbox-focused) — evidence-based provides better assurance of actual security effectiveness
They are identical approaches
Evidence-based is for external audits; compliance-based for internal
questions.explanation: Compliance-based auditing ('checkbox' approach) verifies that required controls are documented and nominally in place. Evidence-based auditing goes further — testing whether controls actually function as intended and produce the desired security outcomes through sampling, technical testing, and validation of effectiveness. Modern audit practice combines both: verify compliance requirements are met AND that controls actually reduce risk effectively.
questions.takeaway: Compliance-based = Controls documented and nominally present; Evidence-based = Controls actually work and reduce risk (combines both for quality audit)

questions.question: What is 'access certification' or 'recertification' in identity governance?
options: Testing users' knowledge of access control policies
Obtaining IT security certifications for access teams

The periodic process of formally reviewing and certifying (approving or revoking) all user access rights by account owners or managers — ensuring access remains appropriate and the principle of least privilege is maintained over time
Certifying new users in security awareness
questions.explanation: Access certification (or access recertification) is the periodic formal review where system or data owners review all user accounts and access rights and certify whether each user still requires their current access. Accounts with unnecessary access are revoked. This process addresses access creep — the gradual accumulation of excessive access rights as users change roles. PCI DSS requires quarterly certification for privileged users; SOX and ISO 27001 require periodic review commensurate with risk.
questions.takeaway: Access Certification = Periodic formal review + certification of all user access rights by data owners; prevents access creep

questions.question: What is a 'compensating control' and give a PCI DSS example?
options: A control that compensates for excessive security spending
A control that financially compensates for security failures
A backup control that activates only when the primary control fails

An alternative control implemented when the primary required control cannot be feasibly implemented, providing equivalent protection. PCI DSS example: a legacy system can't support strong cryptography, so compensating controls might include network isolation, additional monitoring, strict access controls, and more frequent vulnerability scanning
questions.explanation: A compensating control is a PCI DSS mechanism allowing alternative security measures when a specific requirement cannot be directly implemented due to legitimate technical or business constraints. Example: A legacy SCADA system cannot run up-to-date antivirus (PCI DSS Req 5). Compensating controls might include: network isolation of the legacy system, compensating monitoring (IDS/IPS), strict access controls, increased vulnerability assessment frequency, and enhanced logging. All must be formally documented and QSA-approved.
questions.takeaway: Compensating Control (PCI DSS) = Alternative to required control when technically infeasible; must be QSA-approved and formally documented

questions.question: What is the difference between 'security testing' and 'security monitoring'?
options: Security testing is active, periodic evaluation of controls (vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, compliance assessments); Security monitoring is passive, continuous observation of security events and system state — both are required for comprehensive compliance
They are identical activities
Testing uses automated tools; monitoring uses human analysts only
Security testing is for compliance; monitoring is for operations
questions.explanation: Security testing actively evaluates security controls at a point in time — through vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, code review, compliance assessments. It identifies weaknesses. Security monitoring continuously observes system behavior, network traffic, and security events in near-real-time — through SIEM, EDR, NDR, audit log review — to detect ongoing threats and control failures. Both are needed: testing validates control design; monitoring detects operational failures between tests.
questions.takeaway: Security Testing = Active periodic evaluation (pen tests, scans); Security Monitoring = Passive continuous observation (SIEM, EDR). Both required.

questions.question: What is 'ISO/IEC 27019' and who should use it?
options: A physical security standard for power plants

Information security controls for the energy sector — extending ISO 27001/27002 specifically to process control systems (SCADA, DCS, PLCs) used in energy utilities, providing sector-specific guidance for industrial control system security
A general cybersecurity standard for all industries
A cloud security standard for energy companies
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27019 provides information security guidelines for process control systems used in the energy sector (electricity, gas, oil, heating, water). It extends ISO/IEC 27002 with energy-sector-specific controls addressing: process control network security, operational technology (OT) security, safety system interactions, legacy system constraints, and regulatory compliance for energy utilities. It complements NERC CIP for international energy organizations.
questions.takeaway: ISO/IEC 27019 = Information security controls for energy sector process control systems (SCADA, DCS, PLCs); extends ISO 27002 for OT security

questions.question: What is 'NIST SP 800-82'?
options: Cloud security guidelines
Mobile device security standards

Guide to Industrial Control System (ICS) Security — providing guidance for securing SCADA, DCS, PLCs, and other industrial control systems used in critical infrastructure, addressing both cybersecurity and operational safety
Email security guidelines
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-82 (Guide to Industrial Control System Security) provides guidance for securing industrial control systems (ICS) including SCADA, DCS, PLCs, and safety instrumented systems. It addresses the unique security challenges of ICS environments where availability and safety often take precedence over confidentiality, and where patching and traditional IT security practices may not be directly applicable due to operational constraints.
questions.takeaway: NIST SP 800-82 = ICS Security guide for SCADA, DCS, PLCs in critical infrastructure (balances cybersecurity with operational availability/safety)

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'information security awareness week' or regular security communications?
options: Only relevant for new employees
An annual compliance checkbox exercise
A marketing opportunity for security product vendors

Formal programs of regular security communications, reminders, and events that maintain security awareness beyond initial training — building a security culture where security is top-of-mind for all employees throughout the year
questions.explanation: Regular security awareness communications (monthly newsletters, phishing simulations, security tips, awareness events, lunch-and-learns) maintain security as an ongoing priority rather than a once-a-year training event. Research shows that security awareness decays quickly without reinforcement — regular communications help employees retain key security behaviors, adapt to new threats (e.g., AI-powered phishing), and develop a security-conscious mindset. Required by ISO 27001 (Clause 7.3), HIPAA, and PCI DSS.
questions.takeaway: Regular Security Awareness = Maintains security top-of-mind; awareness decays without reinforcement; required by ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS

questions.question: What is 'GDPR enforcement action' and can it happen across EU member states?
options: Enforcement is conducted by national supervisory authorities (SAs) with fines up to €20M or 4% of global turnover; cross-border cases are coordinated through the one-stop-shop mechanism with the lead SA issuing decisions binding on all concerned SAs
Only the European Commission can enforce GDPR
Enforcement is purely voluntary with no real penalties
Enforcement only occurs in the country where the violation occurred
questions.explanation: GDPR enforcement is conducted by national supervisory authorities. For cross-border processing, the lead SA (of the controller's main EU establishment) coordinates enforcement through the cooperation mechanism (Article 60) — consulting all concerned SAs before issuing a final decision. The EDPB can issue binding decisions to resolve disputes between SAs. Major enforcement actions include Meta/Facebook (Ireland's DPC), Amazon (Luxembourg), and Google (France's CNIL).
questions.takeaway: GDPR Enforcement = National SAs with fines up to €20M/4%; cross-border cases led by lead SA; EDPB resolves SA disputes

questions.question: What is a 'security operations center' (SOC) and what are its primary functions?
options: An operations center for physical security guards

A dedicated facility/team that continuously monitors, detects, analyzes, and responds to cybersecurity incidents — using SIEM, EDR, threat intelligence, and skilled analysts operating 24/7/365 to minimize security event dwell time and impact
A compliance reporting center
A secure room for storing sensitive documents
questions.explanation: A Security Operations Center (SOC) is a centralized team (and often dedicated facility) providing continuous cybersecurity monitoring and incident response. Core functions: 24/7 log and alert monitoring, security event triage and analysis, incident response, threat hunting, vulnerability management, threat intelligence consumption, and compliance reporting. SOC teams use SIEM, EDR, NDR, SOAR, and threat intelligence platforms to detect and respond to threats.
questions.takeaway: SOC = 24/7 team monitoring, detecting, analyzing, and responding to security events using SIEM, EDR, SOAR, and threat intelligence

questions.question: What is 'GDPR's impact on marketing and advertising'?
options: GDPR prohibits all marketing to EU residents
GDPR allows marketing if privacy policy is posted

GDPR requires valid consent (or legitimate interests balancing test) for electronic direct marketing, prohibits unsolicited marketing without opt-in, requires easy opt-out mechanisms, and restricts behavioral advertising without explicit consent — significantly impacting digital marketing practices
GDPR only affects B2C marketing, not B2B
questions.explanation: GDPR significantly impacts marketing: email marketing to individuals requires consent (or existing customer relationship with soft opt-in); behavioral/targeted advertising using cookies requires explicit consent (per ePrivacy Directive reinforced by GDPR); profiling for marketing purposes is restricted (legitimate interests must pass balancing test); unsubscribe must be as easy as subscribe; and personal data used for marketing must be processed within the original consent scope. Companies like Google and Meta have faced major GDPR fines for advertising practices.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Marketing Impact = Consent required for email marketing + behavioral ads; easy opt-out; profiling restrictions; Meta/Google fines for violations

questions.question: What is 'security control testing' and what types exist?
options: Testing employee knowledge of security policies
Testing new security tools before purchase
Speed testing of security monitoring systems

Evaluating whether controls are designed appropriately and operating effectively through: inquiry (asking about controls), observation (watching controls operate), inspection (reviewing documentation/configurations), and reperformance (independently executing the control) — providing increasing levels of assurance
questions.explanation: Security control testing evaluates controls using four techniques with increasing assurance levels: (1) Inquiry — asking staff how a control works (weakest evidence); (2) Observation — watching the control operate; (3) Inspection — reviewing evidence (logs, configs, documentation); (4) Reperformance — independently executing the control procedure to verify the outcome (strongest evidence). Auditors combine these techniques, weighting toward stronger evidence for higher-risk controls.
questions.takeaway: Control Testing Techniques (weakest→strongest) = Inquiry → Observation → Inspection → Reperformance

questions.question: What is 'privacy by design' and who coined the term?
options: A foundational privacy concept requiring privacy protections to be embedded into systems and processes from the outset — coined by Ann Cavoukian, former Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, Canada, in the 1990s — now enshrined in GDPR Article 25
A design methodology for private data centers
A software design methodology for private companies
A concept from EU privacy law with no specific author
questions.explanation: Privacy by Design (PbD) was conceptualized by Dr. Ann Cavoukian (former Ontario Privacy Commissioner) in the 1990s as seven foundational principles: proactive not reactive, privacy as default, embedded into design, full functionality, end-to-end security, visibility/transparency, and respect for user privacy. GDPR Article 25 enshrined PbD into law as 'data protection by design and by default,' making these concepts legally required rather than just best practice.
questions.takeaway: Privacy by Design coined by Ann Cavoukian (1990s); 7 principles; enshrined in GDPR Article 25 as legal requirement

questions.question: What is 'IT governance framework' and what are examples?
options: A government framework for IT regulation

A structured set of principles, processes, and accountability mechanisms ensuring IT creates value, manages risk, and uses resources responsibly — examples: COBIT (comprehensive IT governance), ISO/IEC 38500 (governance of IT for the organization), ITIL (service management), and various regulatory governance requirements
A technical framework for IT system architecture
A framework governing IT purchases
questions.explanation: IT governance frameworks provide structure for directing and controlling IT to ensure it supports organizational objectives and manages risk appropriately. Key examples: COBIT 2019 (comprehensive IT governance and management framework by ISACA), ISO/IEC 38500 (international standard for governance of IT for the organization, providing board-level IT governance guidance), and various sector-specific governance requirements (SOX for financial reporting, HIPAA for healthcare governance, PCI DSS for payment governance).
questions.takeaway: IT Governance Frameworks = COBIT (comprehensive), ISO/IEC 38500 (board-level IT governance), ITIL (service management) — ensuring IT creates value and manages risk

questions.question: What is 'key management' and why is it critical for compliance?
options: Managing encryption key employees
Managing passwords for key accounts

The complete lifecycle management of cryptographic keys — including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction — critical because poor key management undermines encryption effectiveness, and is required by PCI DSS Requirement 3, HIPAA, and ISO 27001
IT key card management for physical access
questions.explanation: Cryptographic key management encompasses the entire key lifecycle: secure key generation (using approved algorithms and key lengths), secure key distribution (protecting keys in transit), secure key storage (in HSMs or encrypted key stores), regular key rotation (changing keys before expiration or compromise), key revocation (invalidating compromised keys), and secure key destruction (when no longer needed). PCI DSS Requirement 3.7 mandates key management procedures. Poor key management nullifies the security value of encryption.
questions.takeaway: Key Management = Full lifecycle: Generation → Distribution → Storage → Rotation → Revocation → Destruction (poor management = encryption is useless)

questions.question: What is 'GDPR Article 28' and what does it require?
options: Requirements for data breach notification
Requirements for international data transfers
Requirements for DPO appointment

Requirements for contracts with data processors — mandating written agreements (DPAs) specifying what data processors can do with personal data, ensuring processors only act on controller instructions, and requiring processors to help controllers meet their GDPR obligations
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 28 requires that data controllers use only processors that provide sufficient guarantees about their GDPR compliance, and that all processing by processors be governed by a binding contract (Data Processing Agreement — DPA). The DPA must specify: subject matter, duration, nature and purpose of processing, type of personal data, categories of data subjects, and the processor's obligations including: only processing on controller instructions, ensuring staff confidentiality, implementing security measures, assisting with compliance, and allowing audits.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Article 28 = Mandatory DPA with processors specifying processing scope, processor obligations, and audit rights

questions.question: What is 'DPDPA 2023's approach to children's personal data'?
options: Organizations must obtain verifiable parental consent before processing children's personal data, must not process data in a manner that could harm children's well-being, and must not track children's behavior or target advertising at them — 'verifiable parental consent' is required
No special provisions for children's data
Only health data of children has special protection
Children under 18 cannot use digital services under DPDPA
questions.explanation: DPDPA 2023 Section 9 imposes strict protections for children's personal data (under 18): Data Fiduciaries must obtain verifiable consent of a parent/guardian before processing; must not process data in a manner detrimental to children's well-being; must not track or monitor children; and must not target advertising at children. The Central Government may exempt categories of Data Fiduciaries from parental consent where processing is clearly safe for children.
questions.takeaway: DPDPA 2023 Children's Data = Verifiable parental consent required + No harmful processing + No behavioral tracking + No targeted advertising

questions.question: What is 'ISO/IEC 27001 Clause 9.1' (Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis and Evaluation)?
options: The clause covering management review

The clause requiring organizations to evaluate IS performance and effectiveness — determining what to measure, how to measure it, when to analyze results, and when to communicate results — ensuring the ISMS is actually working as intended through objective measurement
The clause covering internal audit
The clause covering documented information
questions.explanation: ISO 27001 Clause 9.1 requires organizations to monitor, measure, analyze, and evaluate IS performance and ISMS effectiveness. Organizations must determine: what needs to be monitored, the methods for monitoring/measuring/analyzing/evaluating, when it will be performed and by whom, and when results will be analyzed and communicated. Documented information about the results must be retained. ISO 27004 provides guidance on implementing Clause 9.1 measurement programs.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27001 Clause 9.1 = Monitor, measure, analyze, evaluate IS performance with documented methods and retained results

questions.question: What is 'GDPR legitimate interests assessment' (LIA)?
options: A legal opinion letter required for legitimate interests processing
A financial assessment of legitimate business interests

A three-part test for processing under the 'legitimate interests' lawful basis: Purpose test (is the interest legitimate?), Necessity test (is processing necessary to achieve it?), and Balancing test (do the interests override the data subject's rights?) — all three must pass for legitimate interests to apply
An assessment of which interests are legitimate under EU law
questions.explanation: The Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) is the required analysis for GDPR Article 6(1)(f) processing: (1) Purpose test — is there a genuine legitimate interest (commercial, individual, or broader societal)? (2) Necessity test — is processing necessary to achieve the interest, or could a less privacy-invasive means achieve the same result? (3) Balancing test — do the legitimate interests override the data subjects' interests, rights, and freedoms? Organizations must document the LIA outcome.
questions.takeaway: LIA = Three-part test for Legitimate Interests: Purpose (legitimate?) + Necessity (needed?) + Balancing (overrides data subject rights?)

questions.question: What is 'security control effectiveness testing' in ISO 27001 auditing?
options: Testing whether security staff can explain the controls
Testing how many controls have been implemented
Testing whether security controls are expensive enough

Evaluating whether implemented controls are achieving their intended security objectives through sample testing, technical verification, and review of control outputs — not just checking that controls exist on paper
questions.explanation: Control effectiveness testing in ISO 27001 auditing goes beyond documentation review to verify that controls actually achieve their security objectives. For example: not just confirming a patch management policy exists, but testing that systems are actually patched within the policy's defined timeframes; not just confirming access review procedures exist, but verifying that reviews are conducted and inappropriate access is actually revoked. Evidence-based testing of control effectiveness is what distinguishes quality audits.
questions.takeaway: Control Effectiveness Testing = Verify controls ACTUALLY work (not just documented); sample test outputs, verify technical evidence

questions.question: What is 'incident response retainer' and why do organizations use it?
options: A contractual arrangement with an incident response firm providing priority access to their services before an incident occurs — ensuring rapid expert response capacity is available when a breach happens, without delays in procurement
A monthly retainer paid to security staff
A retainer for government incident response teams
A financial retainer for legal counsel after incidents
questions.explanation: An incident response retainer is a pre-negotiated contract with a specialized IR firm that guarantees priority access to their expert team during an incident. Benefits: guaranteed SLA response time, pre-defined scope/pricing, IR firm gains familiarity with your environment during onboarding, and you avoid procurement delays during a high-stress breach. Many cyber insurance policies require or incentivize IR retainers. Required for organizations subject to short breach notification timelines.
questions.takeaway: IR Retainer = Pre-negotiated contract guaranteeing priority expert incident response access before an incident occurs

questions.question: What is 'GDPR Article 13' and when does it apply?
options: Requirements for processor contracts

Transparency information that must be provided to data subjects when their personal data is collected directly from them — covering controller identity, processing purposes, lawful basis, recipients, retention periods, and data subject rights
Requirements for data breach documentation
Requirements for cross-border data transfers
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 13 specifies the information that must be provided to data subjects at the time of collection when data is collected directly from them (e.g., when someone fills out a form). This includes: controller identity and contact details, DPO contact (if applicable), processing purposes and lawful basis, legitimate interests (if used), recipients, cross-border transfer details, retention period, all applicable rights, and whether consent can be withdrawn. This is typically implemented through a privacy notice.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Article 13 = Information to provide when collecting data directly from individuals (privacy notice content requirements)

questions.question: What is a 'table of controls' in an ISMS and how does it differ from the SoA?
options: They are identical documents
The table of controls is the SoA in a different format

A table of controls documents the specific controls selected for implementation; the SoA is a more formal document mapping controls to ISO 27001 Annex A with justification for exclusions — organizations often use both: the table for implementation tracking and the SoA for certification purposes
Only large organizations need a table of controls
questions.explanation: A controls implementation table tracks all selected security controls (from Annex A and additional controls identified through risk treatment) with implementation status, owner, evidence, and test results. The Statement of Applicability (SoA) is the formal ISO 27001 document specifically required for certification, mapping all Annex A controls to applicability status and justifications. Many organizations use both together: the detailed implementation tracking table internally and the SoA for audit/certification.
questions.takeaway: Controls Table = Internal implementation tracker; SoA = Formal ISO 27001 certification document mapping Annex A controls with justification for exclusions

questions.question: What is 'NIST SP 800-190' about?
options: Cloud security fundamentals
Mobile application security
Network infrastructure security

Application Container Security Guide — providing security recommendations for container technologies (Docker, Kubernetes) including image security, registry security, orchestration security, and runtime security
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-190 (Application Container Security Guide) provides guidance for securing container-based deployments. It addresses security considerations for: container images (image vulnerabilities, trusted base images, image scanning), container registries (access controls, image signing), container orchestration (Kubernetes security), container runtime security, and container network security. It helps organizations using Docker/Kubernetes meet security and compliance requirements.
questions.takeaway: NIST SP 800-190 = Container Security Guide (Docker, Kubernetes): image security, registry, orchestration, runtime, and network security

questions.question: What is the 'shared responsibility model' for cloud compliance?
options: Cloud provider is responsible for security OF the cloud (physical infrastructure, hypervisor, network fabric); customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, applications, identity, configurations, access controls) — compliance obligations are divided accordingly
Both parties share equal responsibility for all aspects
Cloud provider handles all compliance for customers
Customer has no compliance obligations in cloud
questions.explanation: The cloud shared responsibility model divides security and compliance obligations: The cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) secures the underlying infrastructure (physical data centers, power, network fabric, hypervisor). The customer secures everything they deploy on the cloud (data encryption, access controls, network configurations, application security, operating system patching for IaaS). For PaaS and SaaS, the boundary shifts — more provider responsibility, less customer responsibility.
questions.takeaway: Shared Responsibility = Provider secures OF cloud (infrastructure); Customer secures IN cloud (data, apps, identity, configs)

questions.question: What does 'ISO 27001 Clause 7.5' (Documented Information) require?
options: All documents must be in paper format

Organizations must create, update, and control documented information — ensuring it is available when needed, adequately protected, properly identified, stored appropriately, and retained and disposed of properly
Documents only need to be accessible to the security team
Only mandatory policies need to be documented
questions.explanation: ISO 27001 Clause 7.5 requires organizations to create and update documented information with appropriate identification, format, and review/approval. Organizations must control documented information to ensure it is available and suitable for use when and where needed, adequately protected (from loss of confidentiality, improper use, loss of integrity), properly distributed, stored and preserved, retained for required periods, and disposed of securely when no longer needed.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27001 Clause 7.5 = Documented information must be: identified, protected, available when needed, retained per schedule, and securely disposed

questions.question: What is 'vendor security questionnaire' (VSQ) in third-party risk management?
options: A survey asking vendors about their product pricing
A performance questionnaire for security tool vendors

A structured set of questions sent to vendors to assess their information security practices, controls, and compliance status before onboarding and periodically thereafter — typically covering areas like access controls, encryption, incident response, certifications, and compliance with regulations
A questionnaire asking vendors about their business goals
questions.explanation: A Vendor Security Questionnaire (VSQ) assesses a third party's security posture before and during a business relationship. Standard VSQs cover: security policies, access controls, encryption, network security, incident response, business continuity, employee training, compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), and regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS). Standardized questionnaires like SIG (Standardized Information Gathering) and CAIQ (Cloud Assessment Initiative Questionnaire) reduce vendor questionnaire fatigue.
questions.takeaway: Vendor Security Questionnaire = Structured assessment of third-party security controls, certifications, and compliance before/during engagement

questions.question: What is 'GDPR's impact on cross-border M&A (mergers and acquisitions)'?
options: Only post-merger compliance is required under GDPR
GDPR prohibits all cross-border M&A activities
GDPR only applies to M&A transactions over a certain size

M&A transactions involving EU companies require GDPR due diligence — assessing target company's data protection compliance, reviewing processing activities and lawful bases, identifying compliance gaps, and understanding potential GDPR liabilities that could affect transaction value
questions.explanation: GDPR significantly impacts M&A involving EU companies: acquirers must conduct GDPR due diligence to: inventory all personal data processed, assess compliance maturity, identify material compliance gaps and breach history, evaluate regulatory risk (pending investigations, complaints), assess contractual obligations with processors/customers, and determine whether the transaction structure itself requires additional consents or notifications. GDPR violations discovered post-acquisition become the acquirer's liability.
questions.takeaway: GDPR in M&A = Due diligence required: data inventory, compliance assessment, gap identification, liability assessment before acquisition

questions.question: What is 'audit sampling' risk and how is it managed?
options: The risk that a sample selected from a population is not representative, leading to incorrect conclusions about the entire population — managed through appropriate sample size, random selection methods, stratification for high-risk items, and documentation of sampling methodology
Risk that samples are too small to be useful
Risk that sample data is compromised during testing
Risk that auditors choose too many samples
questions.explanation: Audit sampling risk is the risk that the auditor's conclusion based on a sample differs from the conclusion that would be reached if the entire population were examined. Management strategies: determine appropriate sample size (larger samples reduce sampling risk), use statistically valid selection methods (random, systematic, stratified), stratify population by risk to focus on high-risk items, and document the sampling plan, method, results, and conclusion rationale.
questions.takeaway: Audit Sampling Risk = Sample conclusions might differ from full population conclusions; managed by sample size, random selection, stratification

questions.question: What is 'COBIT APO02' process?
options: Advanced Performance Operations 2

Managed Strategy — the process covering IT strategy formulation, aligning IT strategy with enterprise strategy, communicating IT strategy, and managing the strategy portfolio of IT-enabled change initiatives
Application Portfolio Optimization process 2
Automated Process Optimization 2
questions.explanation: COBIT APO02 (Managed Strategy) covers the development and maintenance of IT strategy. It includes: understanding the enterprise direction, identifying IT capabilities required to achieve business objectives, analyzing current and future IT capabilities gaps, formulating an IT strategic plan, communicating the strategy, and managing the IT strategy change portfolio. APO02 ensures IT strategy aligns with and enables enterprise strategy.
questions.takeaway: COBIT APO02 = Managed Strategy — IT strategy formulation, alignment with enterprise goals, gap analysis, and communication

questions.question: What is 'ISO/IEC 27050' about?
options: Electronic payment security
Incident response standards

Electronic discovery (eDiscovery) — guidelines for identifying, preserving, collecting, processing, reviewing, and producing electronically stored information (ESI) in legal proceedings or investigations, with information security implications
Email security standards
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27050 (Electronic Discovery) provides guidelines for handling Electronically Stored Information (ESI) in legal proceedings and investigations — covering the eDiscovery lifecycle: identification, preservation, collection, processing, review, analysis, and production of ESI. It addresses information security considerations including: maintaining integrity of evidence, access controls during collection, chain of custody, and protecting privileged or confidential information during the process.
questions.takeaway: ISO/IEC 27050 = Electronic Discovery (eDiscovery) guidelines for ESI handling in legal proceedings with IS security considerations

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'CIS Controls Implementation Group 2 (IG2)'?
options: The same as IG1 but for regulated industries
Provides advanced controls for government agencies only
Only for medium-sized companies

Adds 74 additional safeguards beyond IG1 for organizations that support operations critical to their sector, handle sensitive client/employee data, or have compliance requirements (HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOX) — building on IG1's basic cyber hygiene with more advanced controls
questions.explanation: CIS IG2 targets organizations with dedicated IT staff handling sensitive data or providing services critical to their sector. IG2 adds 74 safeguards to IG1's 56, providing: enhanced vulnerability management, more robust access control, advanced monitoring, application security, security awareness beyond basics, and additional network protection. IG2 is appropriate for organizations subject to HIPAA, PCI DSS, or other compliance mandates requiring more than basic security hygiene.
questions.takeaway: CIS IG2 = IG1 + 74 additional safeguards for organizations with sensitive data, sector criticality, or compliance mandates (HIPAA, PCI DSS)

questions.question: What are 'technical security standards' vs 'information security policies'?
options: Policies state management's high-level intent and mandatory requirements; technical standards provide specific, detailed requirements for how to implement policies (e.g., a password policy states that strong passwords are required; a technical standard specifies minimum 12 characters, complexity requirements, and prohibited patterns)
They are identical documents at different levels
Technical standards are optional; policies are mandatory
Policies are for IT staff; technical standards are for managers
questions.explanation: Information security policies express management's intent and mandatory high-level requirements (e.g., 'The organization shall implement strong authentication for all systems'). Technical security standards provide specific, measurable requirements for implementing policies (e.g., 'Passwords shall be minimum 12 characters, include uppercase, lowercase, number, and special character; dictionary words are prohibited; passwords shall not be reused for 12 previous passwords'). Standards translate policy into actionable technical requirements.
questions.takeaway: Policy = High-level intent (WHAT must be done); Technical Standard = Specific measurable requirements (HOW to do it)

questions.question: What is 'GDPR Article 32' about?
options: Requirements for DPO appointment

Security of processing — requiring controllers and processors to implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure security appropriate to the risk, including pseudonymization, encryption, confidentiality/integrity/availability assurance, and resilience of processing systems
Data breach notification requirements
Cross-border data transfer safeguards
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 32 requires controllers and processors to implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures to protect personal data against the risks of processing. Measures include (not exclusively): pseudonymization and encryption, ensuring ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems, ability to restore access to data in a timely manner after incidents, and a process for regularly testing and evaluating security measures. 'Appropriate' is determined by the risk to individuals.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Article 32 = Appropriate technical and organizational security measures: encryption, pseudonymization, availability, resilience, regular testing

questions.question: What is the 'NIST CSF profile' for a specific sector?
options: A technical profile describing NIST's systems
A NIST employee certification profile

A sector-specific customization of the NIST CSF that establishes expected cybersecurity outcomes for organizations in that sector — examples include profiles for financial services (FS-ISAC), healthcare, and energy — providing a common baseline while allowing for customization
A government-issued compliance profile
questions.explanation: Sector-specific NIST CSF profiles customize the Framework for particular industries by mapping sector-relevant requirements (regulations, standards, threat intelligence) to CSF categories and subcategories. For example, the Financial Services Sector Cybersecurity Profile maps NIST CSF outcomes to FFIEC cybersecurity assessment tool questions. Sector profiles provide a common starting point tailored to the specific threats, regulatory context, and business processes of each industry.
questions.takeaway: Sector-Specific NIST CSF Profile = Industry customization mapping sector regulations and threats to CSF categories (FS-ISAC for finance, etc.)

questions.question: What are 'security controls baselines' in NIST SP 800-53?
options: Baseline salary ranges for security professionals
The minimum performance speed of security tools
Security controls for baseline federal employees

Predefined sets of minimum security controls for federal information systems categorized as LOW, MODERATE, or HIGH impact (per FIPS 199) — providing a starting point for control selection that organizations then tailor for their specific environment
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-53 defines three security control baselines aligned with system impact categorization (FIPS 199): LOW baseline (minimum controls for low-impact systems), MODERATE baseline (additional controls for moderate-impact systems), and HIGH baseline (most comprehensive controls for high-impact systems). Organizations start with the appropriate baseline and then tailor it by: scoping (removing non-applicable controls), compensating (adding alternative controls), and supplementing (adding controls for specific risk).
questions.takeaway: NIST SP 800-53 Baselines = LOW/MODERATE/HIGH control sets aligned with FIPS 199 categorization; tailored through scoping, compensating, supplementing

questions.question: What is 'data breach response' and what are the key steps?
options: A structured process: Detect (identify the breach), Contain (stop ongoing data exposure), Eradicate (remove the threat), Recover (restore systems/data), Notify (regulators, affected individuals per regulatory timelines), and Review (post-incident analysis and improvement)
Only performing forensic investigation
Only notifying customers of a breach
Only changing passwords and updating systems
questions.explanation: Data breach response follows a structured process: (1) Detect — identify and confirm the breach; (2) Contain — stop the ongoing data exposure (isolate systems, revoke compromised credentials); (3) Eradicate — remove the threat (malware, backdoors); (4) Recover — restore systems and verify security; (5) Notify — report to regulators (GDPR 72hrs, HIPAA 60 days, CERT-In 6hrs) and affected individuals; (6) Review — conduct post-incident analysis and implement lessons learned.
questions.takeaway: Data Breach Response = Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Notify (meet regulatory timelines) → Review/Improve

questions.question: What is 'ISO/IEC 27001 risk assessment methodology' and what must it include?
options: Only quantitative risk assessment using financial values

A consistent, reproducible, and comparable risk assessment process — must include: how to identify risks (assets, threats, vulnerabilities), how to analyze risks (likelihood and impact), how to evaluate risks (against risk criteria), and how to prioritize risks for treatment — documented so any assessor produces consistent results
Any spreadsheet-based approach
Only qualitative risk assessment methods
questions.explanation: ISO 27001 Clause 6.1.2 requires a risk assessment methodology that: identifies information security risks (associated with the ISMS scope), analyzes risks (assessing likelihood and impact), evaluates risks (comparing against risk acceptance criteria), and produces consistent, valid, and comparable results when repeated. The methodology must be documented so that different people applying it reach consistent conclusions. Both qualitative and quantitative approaches are acceptable.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27001 Risk Assessment Methodology = Documented, consistent, reproducible process: Identify → Analyze (likelihood × impact) → Evaluate → Prioritize

questions.question: What is the 'COBIT 2019 governance system design' concept?
options: A standard government IT design framework
A design methodology for IT systems

The principle that each organization must design its governance system based on its specific context — using design factors (enterprise strategy, risk profile, size, IT implementation model, etc.) to determine which governance and management objectives are most important and what target capability levels to aim for
A design specification for COBIT software tools
questions.explanation: COBIT 2019 introduced the concept of governance system design — rather than prescribing a one-size-fits-all approach, organizations must design their governance system based on their specific design factors. Using 11 design factors (enterprise strategy, risk profile, threat landscape, compliance requirements, sourcing model, technology adoption strategy, etc.) organizations determine which COBIT objectives matter most, their current capability levels, and target improvement levels.
questions.takeaway: COBIT 2019 Governance System Design = Tailored to organization using 11 design factors; not one-size-fits-all

questions.question: What is the 'principle of professional competence and due care' in IS auditing?
options: Competence means having ISO 27001 Lead Auditor certification only
Auditors must have PhD qualifications
Auditors must always agree with management findings

IS auditors must maintain professional knowledge and skills at a level required to ensure competent professional services, and must act diligently in accordance with applicable standards while performing professional duties
questions.explanation: The IS auditing principle of professional competence and due care requires: maintaining the professional knowledge, skills, and technical competence needed to deliver quality services (achieved through education, training, and experience), and applying diligence, care, and thoroughness when performing audit activities in accordance with applicable professional standards. This principle is embedded in ISACA's Code of Professional Ethics and ITAF standards.
questions.takeaway: IS Auditor Competence and Due Care = Maintain up-to-date knowledge/skills + Apply diligence and thoroughness per professional standards

questions.question: What is 'HIPAA Security Rule administrative safeguard' for workforce training?
options: Organizations must implement security awareness and training programs for all members of the workforce, including periodic security reminders, protection from malicious software, log-in monitoring, and password management guidance
Only technical staff require security training under HIPAA
Training is optional if written policies exist
Annual 1-hour online compliance training only
questions.explanation: HIPAA Security Rule Workforce Security Awareness and Training requires: (1) Security reminders (periodic communications about security), (2) Protection from malicious software (training on recognizing and reporting malware), (3) Log-in Monitoring (training on reviewing log-in attempts and discrepancies), and (4) Password Management (training on creating and protecting passwords). All workforce members — not just IT staff — must receive training appropriate to their role.
questions.takeaway: HIPAA Security Training = For ALL workforce: security reminders + malware awareness + login monitoring + password management

questions.question: What is the PCI DSS 'Account Data' definition?
options: Any data in a bank account

Account Data = Cardholder Data (CHD) + Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD): CHD includes PAN, cardholder name, expiration date, and service code; SAD includes full magnetic stripe/chip data, CAV2/CVV/CVC/CID codes, and PINs
Only the card number and expiration date
Only data stored in payment databases
questions.explanation: PCI DSS defines Account Data as the combination of Cardholder Data (CHD) and Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD). CHD: Primary Account Number (PAN), Cardholder Name, Expiration Date, Service Code. SAD (prohibited from storage after authorization): Full Track Data (magnetic stripe or chip equivalent), CAV2/CVC2/CVV2/CID (card verification codes), PINs/PIN blocks. Only specific CHD elements (not SAD) can be stored with proper protection.
questions.takeaway: PCI DSS Account Data = CHD (PAN, name, expiry, service code) + SAD (track data, CVV/CVC, PINs — NEVER store SAD post-authorization)

questions.question: What is 'NIST SP 800-92' about?
options: Supply chain risk management
Cloud computing security

Guide to Computer Security Log Management — providing guidance on establishing log management policies, implementing efficient log management processes, and analyzing logs for security, operational, and compliance purposes
Network security monitoring
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-92 (Guide to Computer Security Log Management) provides guidance for organizations on log management — establishing policies for what to log, implementing log management infrastructure (centralized logging, SIEM), defining log retention requirements, and using logs for security monitoring, forensic investigations, and compliance demonstration. It complements PCI DSS Requirement 10 and ISO 27001 logging controls.
questions.takeaway: NIST SP 800-92 = Computer Security Log Management guide (policy, infrastructure, retention, analysis for security and compliance)

questions.question: What is the CIS 'IG3' and which organizations should target it?
options: IG3 is for all organizations with more than 1000 employees
IG3 is an optional advanced set for technology companies
IG3 is for government organizations only

IG3 adds 23 additional safeguards to IG1+IG2 (total 153) for organizations with significant cybersecurity expertise that are likely targets of sophisticated adversaries (nation-states, APTs) — including critical infrastructure, defense contractors, and financial institutions
questions.explanation: CIS IG3 (23 additional safeguards beyond IG2, total 153 safeguards) targets organizations that: are likely targets of sophisticated, nation-state-level adversaries; require reduced likelihood of successful attacks against highly sensitive data or critical systems; have the security expertise and resources to implement advanced controls. Target industries: critical infrastructure operators, defense contractors, large financial institutions, government agencies with classified or highly sensitive data.
questions.takeaway: CIS IG3 = 23 additional safeguards (total 153) for high-value targets facing nation-state/APT threats (critical infrastructure, defense, finance)

questions.question: What is 'SOC 2 Type 2 audit period' and what evidence does the auditor examine?
options: Typically 6-12 months; auditors examine evidence of control operation throughout the entire period — including logs, access review documentation, change records, security configurations, training records, and test results — to verify controls operated consistently
A single day's worth of security evidence
Only the last month before the audit completes
A minimum of 3 years of security evidence
questions.explanation: A SOC 2 Type 2 observation period is typically 6 months (minimum) to 12 months. During this period, the auditor collects evidence that controls operated effectively throughout — not just at one point in time. Evidence types: system-generated logs (access logs, change logs), documented control outputs (access reviews, patch reports, vulnerability scan results), configurations, training completion records, and policy documents. The auditor tests samples from throughout the period to assess consistent operation.
questions.takeaway: SOC 2 Type 2 Period = 6-12 months; auditor samples evidence from throughout (logs, reviews, configs, training) to verify consistent control operation

questions.question: What is the 'digital personal data' definition under DPDPA 2023?
options: Only data stored in cloud systems

Personal data in digital form — data about or relating to a natural person who is directly or indirectly identifiable, collected, stored, or processed in digital format, including data originally in non-digital form that is subsequently digitized
All types of data processed by computers
Only data collected from websites and mobile apps
questions.explanation: DPDPA 2023 defines 'digital personal data' as data about or relating to a natural person who is directly or indirectly identifiable, and which is in digital form. Importantly, it also covers data that was originally in non-digital form but was subsequently digitized. This broad definition mirrors GDPR's approach to personal data, ensuring the Act covers all personal data that enters digital systems regardless of its original format.
questions.takeaway: DPDPA 2023 Digital Personal Data = Data about identifiable individuals in digital form, including originally non-digital data that is subsequently digitized

questions.question: What is 'information security policy' and what should it contain?
options: A list of all security tools used by the organization
An annual compliance declaration for regulators

A high-level document approved by top management expressing the organization's commitment to information security, its objectives, definition of scope, principles, responsibilities, and reference to supporting standards and procedures
A detailed technical manual for the security team
questions.explanation: An information security policy is the foundational governance document expressing top management's commitment to and direction for information security. It should include: statement of management commitment to security, definition of IS and its objectives, scope of the ISMS, assignment of general responsibilities, reference to supporting standards/procedures/guidelines, and compliance requirements. ISO 27001 Clause 5.2 requires this policy to be documented, communicated, and available to stakeholders.
questions.takeaway: IS Policy = Top management commitment document with: objectives, scope, responsibilities, compliance requirements, and references to standards

questions.question: What is 'GDPR Data Protection by Design' (DPbD) practical implementation?
options: A design review conducted after system deployment
Only relevant for software developers
Designing privacy-themed office decoration

Implementing privacy-protective technical and organizational measures from the earliest stages of system/product development — including: data minimization in data models, default access controls restricting unnecessary access, encryption built-in from the start, and privacy impact assessment before development begins
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 25's Data Protection by Design requires practical implementation throughout the development lifecycle: conduct privacy impact assessments before starting development; minimize data collection in requirements/data models; implement access controls and encryption as foundational architecture decisions; use privacy-enhancing technologies; test privacy controls before deployment; and train developers on secure/privacy-aware coding. PbD must be embedded in the software development lifecycle (SDLC), not added as an afterthought.
questions.takeaway: DPbD Implementation = DPIA before design + data minimization + built-in encryption + access controls + PETs + privacy testing in SDLC

questions.question: What is 'IT audit risk-based approach' and why is it preferred?
options: Prioritizing audit resources on areas with highest inherent risk, strategic importance, regulatory significance, or history of control failures — ensuring audit effort focuses where it adds most value rather than spreading evenly across all areas regardless of risk
Auditing areas with highest probability of finding something
Risk-based means only auditing after incidents occur
Conducting audits only when management requests them
questions.explanation: A risk-based audit approach allocates limited audit resources where they provide maximum assurance value — focusing on high-risk processes, critical systems, regulatory-sensitive areas, and areas with previous control failures. This contrasts with a coverage-based approach that audits all areas equally regardless of risk. Risk-based auditing better aligns with the board's need for assurance about the risks that matter most, and is mandated by IIA standards and ISACA's ITAF.
questions.takeaway: Risk-Based Audit = Resources focused on highest-risk, highest-value areas (regulatory, strategic, control failure history) not equal coverage

questions.question: What is 'patch priority' in vulnerability management and what frameworks guide it?
options: Apply all patches on the same monthly schedule

Prioritizing patches based on vulnerability severity (CVSS score), asset criticality, and exploitability — PCI DSS requires critical vulnerabilities (CVSS ≥7) within 30 days; NIST recommends risk-based prioritization considering exploitability and impact; CIS Control 7 requires continuous vulnerability management
Only patch internet-facing systems
Patch all systems alphabetically
questions.explanation: Patch prioritization balances urgency (vulnerability severity/exploitability) with asset criticality. PCI DSS Requirement 6.3 requires high-risk and critical patches within defined timeframes (typically 30 days for critical). NIST encourages risk-based prioritization using CVSS scores, CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, and asset criticality. CIS Control 7 emphasizes continuous vulnerability management with risk-based remediation timelines aligned to severity.
questions.takeaway: Patch Priority = CVSS severity × Asset criticality × Exploitability; PCI DSS requires critical patches within 30 days

questions.question: What is a 'security dashboard' and how does it support compliance?
options: A physical display showing server temperatures
A regulatory reporting portal

A visual management tool displaying key security metrics, risk indicators, compliance status, and trending data in real-time — enabling executives and boards to understand security posture at a glance and supporting evidence-based compliance reporting
A technical monitoring tool for security analysts only
questions.explanation: A security dashboard visualizes key security metrics and compliance status — open vulnerabilities by severity, patching compliance rate, security incidents/trends, control effectiveness indicators, training completion rates, and regulatory compliance status. Dashboards support compliance by: providing continuous visibility into compliance posture, enabling proactive risk management, generating evidence for auditors, and giving boards the visibility needed for security governance. ISO 27001 Clause 9.1 requires measurement of IS performance.
questions.takeaway: Security Dashboard = Visual KPIs and KRIs for real-time security posture visibility; supports governance, compliance evidence, and board reporting

questions.question: What is 'ISACA ITAF' (Information Technology Assurance Framework)?
options: An IT authentication framework for financial systems
A government assurance framework for IT vendors
A financial IT assurance framework

ISACA's comprehensive framework setting professional standards, guidelines, and tools for IS auditors — encompassing IS Audit Standards (mandatory), IS Audit Guidelines (best practices), and IS Audit Tools and Techniques (practical guidance) — providing the professional framework for the IS audit profession
questions.explanation: ISACA's Information Technology Assurance Framework (ITAF) is the professional framework governing IS audit practice. It has three tiers: IS Audit and Assurance Standards (mandatory — define minimum requirements and must be followed), IS Audit and Assurance Guidelines (guidance on how to apply standards — best practices), and IS Audit and Assurance Tools and Techniques (practical examples and templates). ITAF aligns with IIA standards for integrated audit practice.
questions.takeaway: ISACA ITAF = 3 tiers: Standards (mandatory minimum requirements) + Guidelines (best practices) + Tools and Techniques (practical guidance)

questions.question: What is 'GDPR supervisory authority consultation' (Article 36)?
options: When a DPIA indicates processing would result in a HIGH residual risk (after mitigation), the controller must consult the supervisory authority before proceeding — the SA then has up to 8 weeks to provide written advice
Consulting supervisory authorities before any data collection
Optional consultation when controllers have questions about GDPR
Consulting with supervisory authorities on annual compliance plans
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 36 (Prior Consultation) requires controllers to consult the supervisory authority BEFORE beginning processing when a DPIA indicates the processing would result in a high risk (despite implementing mitigating measures). The SA then has up to 8 weeks (extendable by 6 more weeks for complex cases) to provide written advice. If the SA considers processing would violate GDPR, it may prohibit the processing or provide remediation guidance.
questions.takeaway: GDPR Article 36 = Prior consultation with SA required when DPIA shows HIGH residual risk; SA has 8 weeks to advise

questions.question: What is 'asset classification' in ISO 27001 and why is it foundational?
options: Classifying IT assets by purchase price

Categorizing information assets based on their value, sensitivity, and criticality to the organization — foundational because classification determines the level of protection required, guides risk assessment prioritization, and informs control selection for each asset type
An HR classification of employee assets
Classifying physical assets for insurance purposes
questions.explanation: Asset classification categorizes information assets (data, systems, applications, services) by their value and sensitivity to the organization. Classification levels (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Highly Confidential) determine: required access controls (who can access), storage requirements (where and how securely), transmission requirements (whether encryption is required), and disposal requirements (how to destroy securely). Without classification, resources cannot be protected appropriately relative to their value.
questions.takeaway: Asset Classification = Categorize assets by value/sensitivity → determines access controls, storage, transmission, and disposal requirements

questions.question: What is the 'third-party audit' in the context of ISO 27001 certification?
options: A customer audit of the organization's security controls
A regulatory inspection of the ISMS

An audit conducted by an independent accredited certification body — external to both the organization (second party) and distinct from internal audit (first party) — resulting in ISO 27001 certificate if conformance is confirmed
An audit conducted by the organization's internal audit team
questions.explanation: In the context of ISO 27001, audit types are: First-party = internal audit by the organization itself; Second-party = audit by an interested party such as a customer or regulator; Third-party = audit by an independent, accredited certification body (like DNV, BSI, SGS, Bureau Veritas). Only a third-party audit by an accredited certification body can result in ISO 27001 certification, providing independent external assurance of ISMS conformance.
questions.takeaway: ISO 27001 Audit Types: 1st party = Internal, 2nd party = Customer/Regulator, 3rd party = Accredited Certification Body (only 3rd party = ISO 27001 certificate)

questions.question: What is 'continuous compliance monitoring' and how does it differ from periodic compliance assessment?
options: Periodic assessment is always more accurate than continuous monitoring
Continuous monitoring is only for cloud environments
They are identical approaches

Continuous monitoring uses automated tools to check compliance status in real-time or near-real-time against defined controls — identifying deviations immediately; periodic assessment evaluates compliance at a point in time (annually, quarterly) — continuous provides earlier detection and smaller windows of non-compliance
questions.explanation: Continuous compliance monitoring uses automated tools (GRC platforms, cloud security tools, configuration management tools) to constantly check control status against compliance requirements. When a deviation occurs (e.g., a system falls out of CIS benchmark compliance, a user gains inappropriate access), it is detected and alerted immediately. Periodic assessment (annual SOC 2 audit, annual PCI DSS assessment) only captures state at a point in time — deviations between assessments may go undetected for months.
questions.takeaway: Continuous Monitoring = Real-time automated deviation detection; Periodic Assessment = Point-in-time snapshot (deviations may go undetected between assessments)

questions.question: What is 'cybersecurity insurance underwriting' and what factors affect premiums?
options: Assessment process for cyber liability insurance where insurers evaluate: security controls maturity, incident history, revenue/data volumes, industry sector risk, MFA implementation, backup practices, and patch management — determining coverage and premium based on cyber risk profile
A standard contract provided by all insurance companies
Insurance for IT equipment against physical damage
Only revenue and company size determine cyber insurance premiums
questions.explanation: Cyber insurance underwriting evaluates an organization's cyber risk profile to determine coverage terms and premiums.
